title: Hear Paul McCartneyãs Forgotten 1967 Movie Soundtrack, Arranged by George Martin
description:
in Film, Music |   	March 17th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

In 1967, a young Roger Ebert drew up a top-ten-films-of-the-year list includôÙing Bon
body:
in Film, Music |   	March 17th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

In 1967, a young Roger Ebert drew up a top-ten-films-of-the-year list includôÙing BonôÙnie and Clyde, Blow-Up, The GradôÙuôÙate, A Man for All SeaôÙsons, and Cool Hand Luke. LatôÙer, he added a few more picôÙtures from this cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic bumper crop that he rememôÙbered fondôÙly, the first of which was The FamôÙiôÙly Way. Though selôÙdom refôÙerôÙenced today, it was a big hit in Britain ã one of sevôÙerôÙal, in fact, for the twin-brothôÙer filmôÙmakôÙers John and Roy BoultôÙing. ResponôÙsiôÙble for such nineôÙteen-fifties comeôÙdies as Lucky Jimô andô Iãm All Right Jack, the two attained in their homeôÙland not only auteur staôÙtus, but also the curiôÙous posiôÙtion ofô estabôÙlishôÙment satirists, valôÙiôÙdatôÙing the instiôÙtuôÙtions of mid-cenôÙtuôÙry EngôÙlish life even as they ridiculed them.
AdaptôÙed from a stage play byô Alfieô author Bill Naughton, The FamôÙiôÙly Way finds its mateôÙrôÙiôÙal in the triôÙals of a pair of northôÙern newôÙlyôÙweds who, havôÙing been fleeced by a crooked travôÙel agent, end up havôÙing to spend their honôÙeyôÙmoon at home. Whatãs worse, givôÙen their impeôÙcuôÙniousôÙness, ãhomeã meant a room in the house of the groomãs parôÙents.
That 1967 was a difôÙferôÙent time is also sigôÙnaled by a scene in which the father-in-law belôÙlows for his chamôÙber pot, which his wife had hoped to keep hidôÙden from her new daughôÙter-in-lawãs senôÙsiôÙtive eyes. In that role is the acclaimed perôÙformer of EngôÙlish everyôÙman John Mills, appearôÙing onscreen for the first time with his daughôÙter HayôÙley, who plays the bride. It marked her first real adult part, a kind of gradôÙuôÙaôÙtion from her child-actress career in picôÙtures like The ParôÙent Trapô andô That Darn Cat!
The picôÙture also boastôÙed a score by Paul McCartôÙney, or at any rate by BeaôÙtôÙles proôÙducôÙer George MarôÙtin, who built upon what themes he could sucôÙcessôÙfulôÙly imporôÙtune the seemôÙingôÙly writerãs-blocked BeaôÙtôÙle to bang out. TakôÙing into account that this was hapôÙpenôÙing between Revolverô andô Sgt. PepôÙper, itãs perôÙhaps underôÙstandôÙable that McCartôÙney would feel his creôÙative enerôÙgies drained by othôÙer projects, but the BoultôÙing brothôÙers had offered a first, irreôÙsistible opporôÙtuôÙniôÙty to comôÙpose offiôÙcialôÙly outôÙside the Lennon-McCartôÙney dyad. Though not withôÙout the charms of MarôÙtôÙinãs orchesôÙtral work (more of which would be heard in YelôÙlow SubôÙmaôÙrineô in 1969), The FamôÙiôÙly Wayãs brief soundôÙtrack bears few obviôÙous marks of the McCartôÙney musiôÙcal senôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty. Present on the BeaôÙtôÙlesã albums, of course, that senôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty has conôÙtinôÙued to develôÙop throughôÙout a solo career that hasô outôÙlived the band by 56 years ã and countôÙing.
via BoingôÙBoôÙing

title: Enchanting Video Shows How Globes Were Made by Hand in 1955: The End of a 500-Year Tradition
description:
in Maps |   	March 17th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The first globeãa spherôÙiôÙcal repôÙreôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion of our planôÙet Earthãdates back to the Age
body:
in Maps |   	March 17th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The first globeãa spherôÙiôÙcal repôÙreôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion of our planôÙet Earthãdates back to the Age of DisôÙcovôÙery. Or 1492, to be more preôÙcise, when MarôÙtin Behaimô and painter Georg GlockôÙendon creôÙatôÙed the ãNû¥rnôÙberg TerôÙresôÙtriôÙal Globe,ã othôÙerôÙwise known as the ãErdapfel.ã It was made by hand. And that traôÙdiôÙtion conôÙtinôÙued straight through the 20th cenôÙtuôÙry, until machines largeôÙly took over.
Above, you can watch the wanôÙing of a 500-year traôÙdiôÙtion. SomeôÙwhere in North LonôÙdon, in 1955, ãa woman takes one of the moulds from a shelf and takes it over to a workôÙbench. She fixôÙes it to a device which holds it steady whilst still allowôÙing it to spin.ã ãAnothôÙer girl,ã notes British Pathe, ãis stickôÙing red strips onto a largôÙer sphere.ã After that,ô ãcoloured printôÙed secôÙtions showôÙing the map of the world are cut to shape then pastôÙed onto the surôÙface of the globes.ã Through that ãskilled operôÙaôÙtion,ãô the LonôÙdon-based firm proôÙduced someô 60,000 globes each year.


	

Above, you can also find anothôÙer globe-makôÙing mini-docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary, this one in black & white, from 1949. It gives you a glimpse of a process that takes 15 hours, from start to finôÙish. FinalôÙly, furôÙther down, watch a short feaôÙture on BellerôÙby & Co., which still makes high-qualôÙiôÙty, handôÙcraftôÙed globes in LonôÙdon. Itãs a reminder that, even in the age of mass proôÙducôÙtion, some artiôÙsans are keepôÙing a cenôÙturies-long craft alive.


	

If you would like to sign up for Open Cultureãs free email newsletôÙter,ô please find it here. Itãs a great way to see our new posts, all bunôÙdled in one email, each day.
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!

title: The Fascinating Engineering of the Titanic: How the Great Ocean Liner Was Built
description:
in History, Technology |   	March 16th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When many of us first learned of the RMS TitanôÙic, it was preôÙsentôÙed first
body:
in History, Technology |   	March 16th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When many of us first learned of the RMS TitanôÙic, it was preôÙsentôÙed first as one of hisôÙtoôÙryãs greatôÙest ironies: the ãunsinkôÙableã ocean linôÙer that went down on its maidôÙen voyôÙage. Of course, thereãs a great deal more to the stoôÙry, as anyôÙone who becomes obsessed with the ill-fatôÙed shipô (James Cameron being just one notable examôÙple) underôÙstands full well. Even apart from the many human expeôÙriôÙences surôÙroundôÙing it,ô some of them told by the wreckôÙãs surôÙvivors and preôÙserved on film, the mechanôÙiôÙcal aspects of theô TitanôÙic hold out conôÙsidôÙerôÙable fasôÙciôÙnaôÙtion for anyôÙone with an engiôÙneerãs cast of mind. Put aside, for the moment, the matôÙter of the sinkôÙing, and conôÙsidôÙer just what went into makôÙing it one of the most gloôÙriôÙous creôÙations of man launched into the ocean to date ã or rather, one of the three most gloôÙriôÙous.
The TitanôÙic was one of a trio of simôÙiôÙlar White Star Line ships comôÙpletôÙed in the earôÙly nineôÙteen-tens. In the video above, Bill HamôÙmack, known on YouTube as EngiôÙneerôÙguy, tells the stoôÙry of not just theô TitanôÙic, but also theô Olympic and the HMHS BriôÙtanôÙnic. An engiôÙneerôÙing proôÙfesôÙsor at the UniôÙverôÙsiôÙty of IlliôÙnois, he found in the camôÙpus library issues of the jourôÙnalô The EngiôÙneer pubôÙlished between 1909 and 1911 that conôÙtain detailed phoôÙtographs of the conôÙstrucôÙtion of bothô theô TitanôÙic andô Olympic, sisôÙter ships that were built side-by-side.
One eleôÙment highôÙlightôÙed that we may not much conôÙsidôÙer today is the sheer scale of the things: each was held togethôÙer by three milôÙlion rivôÙets, could conôÙtain 1.5 milôÙlion galôÙlons of balôÙlast water, weighed 52,000 tons when fulôÙly fitôÙted, required 23 tons of lubriôÙcant to slide from the dock into the water, and burnedô 650 tons ofô coal per day on a transatôÙlantic crossôÙing.
Alas, size alone wasôÙnãt enough to preôÙvent disôÙasôÙter. ãLess than a year after the launch of these two giant ships, one sufôÙfered a colôÙliôÙsion that ripped a gapôÙing hole in its side,ã says HamôÙmack. ãThat ship was of course, the Olympic.ã Its sudôÙden encounter with a passôÙing warôÙship necesôÙsiôÙtatôÙed patchôÙing with wood before it could return home for a full repair, but thereôÙafter it remained in serôÙvice for nearôÙly a quarôÙter-cenôÙtuôÙry. Its less lucky sibôÙlingô endôÙed up at the botôÙtom of the ocean after runôÙning into trouôÙble of its own: a mine and a torôÙpeôÙdo spelled the end forô the BriôÙtanôÙnicô in 1916. As for theô TitanôÙic, we all know about its fateôÙful encounter with the iceôÙberg, and maybe weãve even heard disôÙcusôÙsions of how its designôÙers could have mitôÙiôÙgatôÙed the impact: more or taller bulkôÙheads, a douôÙble hull rather than just a douôÙble botôÙtom, greater lifeboat capacôÙiôÙty. As for whether and how those soluôÙtions would have worked, perôÙhaps HamôÙmack could still shoot a folôÙlow-up explainôÙing it all to us.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:

The SinkôÙing of the BriôÙtanôÙnic: An AniôÙmatôÙed IntroôÙducôÙtion to the TitanôÙicãs ForôÙgotôÙten SisôÙter Ship
How a 16th-CenôÙtuôÙry Explorerãs SailôÙing Ship Worked: An AniôÙmatôÙed Video Takes You on a ComôÙpreôÙhenôÙsive Tour
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Extreme Macro Photos of Insect Wings by Chris Perani Layer Thousands of Images
description: For Chris Perani, the most diminutive detailsãthe kind that are virtually impossible to see with the naked eyeãare an endless source of wonder. Hi
body:
For Chris Perani, the most diminutive detailsãthe kind that are virtually impossible to see with the naked eyeãare an endless source of wonder. His ongoing series, Wings, focuses on the prismatic effect of insectsã anatomy in what he describes as ãextreme macro.ã The images reveal details weãd otherwise only be able to see clearly beneath a microscope, and a meticulous process illuminates undulating, scaled surfaces that resemble chromatic pixels, stained glass, or even beadwork.
Perani uses special lenses that magnify objects up to 10 times, but he also takes up to 2,000 carefully measured shots of each specimen. He then digitally stacks them to achieve incredible clarity and dimension. Each high-resolution image is captured at 10-micron intervalsãa distance shorter than the width of a human hairãso precision is paramount.
Bee
The organic architecture of beesã wings, plus those of wasps, damselflies, beetles, and butterflies, illustrates the precision of their anatomy and the way a prism of hues is produced by both pigmentation and structural color, like iridescence.
ãWith many of these insects, light completely changes the result,ã Perani says. ãBees, for example, often have wings that appear dark and colorless at first glance. But when light hits them at exactly the right angle, thin-film interference suddenly reveals remarkable colors, textures, and intricate structures across the wingãs surface, turning what first appears dark into a delicate fabric of light and structure.ã
See more on Peraniãs Instagram.
Male Valanga nigricornis
Madagascan sunset moth
Jeweled flower mantis
Papilio ulysses

Xylocopa latipes
Damselfly

title: Myth, Masks, and LEGO: Ekow Nimako's Elaborate Afrofuturistic Sculptures
description: Mythology, landscapes, and technology converge in the meticulous, Afrofuturistic sculptures of Ekow Nimako. Using thousands of black LEGO bricks,
body:
Mythology, landscapes, and technology converge in the meticulous, Afrofuturistic sculptures of Ekow Nimako. Using thousands of black LEGO bricks, the Ghanaian-Canadian artist explores legends and folklore of the African diaspora, creating figurative embodiments of allegorical creatures and spiritual beings. Through a single, modular medium, he highlights a wide range of cultural phenomena, from graffiti writing in his series Building Black GRAPHICA to sprawling metropolises in Building Black CIVILIZATIONS. 
Recently, Nimako has been working on a collection inspired by African ceremonial masks, interstellar travel, machines, and geometric forms. Some of the works seen here are currently on view in Building Black AMORPHIA: Spiritual Starships at Harbourfront Centre, which is part of a program celebrating Black culture called ZUUMBA 365.

Nimako recently installed a multi-part piece titled ãChildren of the Rouge Valleyã at the Rouge Valley Community Recreation Centre in Scarborough, Ontario, and heãs currently working toward another public installation on a Canadian university campus. Building Black AMORPHIA: Spiritual Starships continues in Toronto through August 31. Find more on the artistãs Instagram, including updates about forthcoming e-store drops.

schema.headline: Myth, Masks, and LEGO: Ekow Nimako's Elaborate Afrofuturistic Sculptures

title: Photographer Dr. Elliot McGucken Seizes a Rare Superbloom in Death Valley
description: Along with its reputation as the driest and lowest national park in the U.S., Death Valley is also one of the hottest places on Earth. It holds th
body:
Along with its reputation as the driest and lowest national park in the U.S., Death Valley is also one of the hottest places on Earth. It holds the air temperature record of 134 degrees Fahrenheit, recorded in 1913. But in spite of its macabre name, Death Valley is anything but lifeless. And over the past couple of weeks, a rare ãsuperbloomã demonstrates just how vivacious it can be.
For photographer Dr. Elliot McGucken, who focuses on landscapes and nature, the visual cacophony of wildflowers foregrounding the Mesquite Flat Sand Dunes or dramatic mountains like Telescope Peak are an endless source of fascination. Death Valleyãs predominant flower displays are magenta and yellowãPhacelia and Desert Gold, respectively. There are also Mojave Stars, Brown-eyed Primrose, Five Spot, Sand Verbena, Brittlebush, and the uniquely named Gravel Ghost.

McGucken was photographing at Yellowstone National Park when he learned that Death Valley had experienced record rainfall over the past few months and was primed for a superbloom. Not wanting to miss it, as they only happen occasionallyãthe last ones occurred in 2016, 2005, and 1998ãhe drove to California. ãAs nature photographers, weãre oft waiting for those ãhell yesã moments to pack up and move out, and this was a ãhell yesã moment!ã McGucken says. ãI traveled from minus 10 degrees Fahrenheit to over 90 degrees in a day!ã
At the beginning of March, Death Valley National Park announced it was experiencing its best superbloom in a decade. At lower elevations, the wildflowers blossom in early March, and depending the weather, can last until mid-month. At higher elevations, wildflowers bloom from April to June, depending on the altitude. 
ãWhile I had photographed a smaller bloom in the Panamint Valley back in 2024, I had never seen so much greenery in the lower parts of the park, from Stovepipe Wells to the Mesquite Dunes to Furnace Creek to Badwater,ã McGucken says. ãI have been photographing for the last few days, making the most of the once-in-decade event.ã
The photos here were taken over the course of several days, including March 16. While the superbloom is now considered past peak, there are still some displays in certain areas of the park. See more on McGuckenãs Instagram, and purchase prints on his website.

schema.headline: Photographer Dr. Elliot McGucken Seizes a Rare Superbloom in Death Valley
